titleStmt.title: Letter from Captain James Davidson to his mother Clara Mary Davidson, 7 December 1915
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: James Davidson

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from James Davidson (1877-1916) to his mother, Clara. Davidson writes a short note to his mother that he has had unofficial news that his Battalion will be sent to the front line. He asks her to dispatch a bag to him as soon as possible so he has it before he leaves.This letter is part of a large correspondence from Captain James Davidson to his parents. Davidson, who was Managing Director of the Sirocco Engineering Works in Belfast, served with 13 Royal Irish Rifles and the Machine Gun Corps. His letters describe his experiences while training in England and serving in France. He was killed on 1 July 1916, the first day of the Battle of the Somme.

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
7/12/15 My dear mother, I posted a letter to you today
but I have since heard unofficially that we
are not to be here much longer & will be
leaving for some part of the line at very
short notice, so this is just a hurried note
to ask you to hurry Erskine with my green
canvas kit bag & have it dispatched
as quickly as possible. I am afraid
that even as it is, it can hardly be here
in time for our move, however perhaps we
may not be off so soon as at present anticipated.
I suspect we shall go to one of the 'Hot Spots' this
time at least that seems to be the general
impression. Of course wherever we are
things will reach me all right, only if possible
I would like the bag before we move. In haste your loving son Jim
On Active Service
Rced 14th Dec/15 Mrs. S. C. Davidson Seacourt Bangor Co. Down Ireland JS Davidson
